summary: Another wild day across the BeltLine corridor and ITP. Decatur's heated up over that controversial learning center while the BeltLine dropped some major Murphy Crossing updates. Meanwhile, Atlanta airport turned into absolute chaos with 2+ hour TSA lines.

topStory.headline: BeltLine's Murphy Crossing Finally Gets Fresh Vision After Last Year's Mess
topStory.summary: Fourteen months after Murphy Crossing plans spectacularly fell apart in public, Atlanta BeltLine Inc. is back with a completely new design. The 20-acre historic site in Oakland City adjacent to the Westside Trail now calls for 600+ housing units and a retail village. This is huge news for anyone who's been waiting to see what happens to that prime real estate.
topStory.body:
Remember when Murphy Crossing imploded last year? Yeah, that was painful to watch. But the BeltLine folks clearly went back to the drawing board in a big way, and what they're showing now looks pretty promising for Oakland City.

The new vision calls for more than 600 housing units across the 20-acre site, plus a retail and office village. What's interesting is they're required by city code to include light industrial uses, which should help preserve some of the area's working character. The Beltline's working with their usual crew at Perkins + Will on this one.

This is exactly the kind of development Oakland City needs to see along the Westside Trail. The question now is whether they can actually pull it off this time without another public meltdown. Given how badly the last attempt went, everyone's going to be watching this one closely.
topStory.imageAlt: New Murphy Crossing development rendering showing mixed-use buildings

stories.headline: Mayor Dickens Breaks Ground on Trinity Center Flats Downtown
stories.summary: Mayor Andre Dickens joined developers Monday to celebrate the groundbreaking of Trinity Center Flats at 104 Trinity Avenue SW. The mixed-use project is another big step for the South Downtown corridor and addressing affordable housing shortages. The location puts residents right across from downtown transit options.
stories.body:
Even with the rain coming down, Mayor Dickens and the Radiant Development Partners team were out there getting dirty at 104 Trinity Avenue SW. Trinity Center Flats represents exactly the kind of mixed-use development South Downtown needs to see more of.

What makes this project particularly smart is the location. Residents are going to be walking distance to major transit connections, jobs, and services. That's the kind of transit-oriented development that actually makes sense for people who want to live car-free or car-light in Atlanta.

The affordable housing component is crucial too. With housing costs going crazy across ITP, having new affordable options in such a connected location could be a real game-changer for working families who want to stay in the city.
stories.imageAlt: Mayor Andre Dickens at Trinity Center Flats groundbreaking ceremony

stories.headline: Decatur Coalition Demands Archaeological Study Before School Construction
stories.summary: The Beacon Hill Grassroots Coalition is calling for a full archaeological survey of the greenspace at 346 W. Trinity Place before Decatur Schools moves ahead with their controversial $22 million Early Childhood Learning Center. The group says the site has deep history involving the Muskogee Creek Nation and enslaved African Americans that needs to be preserved.
stories.body:
This whole Decatur learning center saga just keeps getting more heated. Now the Beacon Hill folks are bringing up the site's history with both the Muskogee Creek Nation and enslaved African Americans, saying there could be important archaeological remains that would be lost if construction moves forward.

Wanda Watters, who's descended from the old Beacon Hill community, put it perfectly: just because something isn't visible today doesn't mean it should be lost to history. That's exactly the kind of perspective that gets overlooked in these development fights.

Meanwhile, Decatur Schools are clearly frustrated. They denied having plans for an immediate groundbreaking last week, but legislators are now demanding a public referendum before any construction starts. This is turning into exactly the kind of messy public battle that nobody wanted but probably should have seen coming.
stories.imageAlt: Site of proposed Early Childhood Learning Center in Decatur

stories.headline: South Peachtree Creek Trail Scores Cash for Major Expansion
stories.summary: The Decide DeKalb Development Authority approved funding last week for sections three and five of the popular trail that connects Emory with Mason Mill Park and other greenspaces. The 2.6-mile existing trail through forests and wetlands is getting extended to provide better off-street access to schools, jobs, and retail between I-85 and Emory.
stories.body:
This is exactly the kind of trail expansion that makes sense for our area. The South Peachtree Creek Trail is already popular with folks who know about it, winding through some beautiful forest and wetland areas between Emory and Mason Mill Park.

What's smart about this expansion is the focus on connectivity. Getting people off-street access to schools, job centers, and retail between I-85 and Emory fills in some real gaps in our pedestrian infrastructure. Too much of metro Atlanta still requires driving everywhere.

Decide DeKalb stepping up with funding shows they get that these trail investments pay off long-term. The existing 2.6 miles prove there's demand for this kind of infrastructure. Now they just need to execute the expansion without the usual delays and cost overruns we see with these projects.
stories.imageAlt: Hikers on South Peachtree Creek Trail at Mason Mill Park

stories.headline: Hartsfield-Jackson Turns Into TSA Nightmare with 2+ Hour Waits
stories.summary: Travelers faced absolutely brutal security lines Monday and Tuesday morning, with wait times hitting over two hours. The mess comes from a perfect storm of weather-related flight cancellations, TSA staffing issues during the government shutdown, and thousands of stranded passengers. Some lines snaked all the way back to baggage claim.
stories.body:
If you've been through Hartsfield-Jackson lately, you know it's been rough. But Monday and Tuesday took it to a whole new level of chaos. We're talking security lines that went way beyond the usual cordoned areas and stretched back to baggage claim. Some folks waited over 127 minutes just to get through TSA.

The timing couldn't be worse. You've got the partial government shutdown affecting TSA staffing, then add severe weather that canceled over 1,000 flights on Monday alone. That left thousands of passengers scrambling to rebook, all hitting security at the same time.

College student Talia Mayes heading on spring break called it "insane and stressful," which pretty much sums up the vibe. Delta even requested ground stops to manage the operational chaos. If you're flying out of Atlanta this week, definitely budget extra time and maybe bring snacks.
stories.imageAlt: Long TSA security lines at Atlanta airport

stories.headline: Viral Breakfast Chain Eggslut Cracks Into Atlanta at Moxy Hotel
stories.summary: The LA-based breakfast phenomenon officially opened March 5 inside the new Moxy Atlanta Downtown hotel, marking their first Georgia location and first in the South. The counter-service spot in the hotel lobby serves their famous brioche buns and soft-scrambled eggs all day, with late-night hours on weekends.
stories.body:
Finally, all that Eggslut hype is available right here in Downtown. The LA chain that went viral for those glossy brioche sandwiches and perfect soft-scrambled eggs set up shop in the Moxy hotel lobby on March 5.

What's smart about this location is the all-day breakfast concept with extended late-night hours on Fridays and Saturdays. Downtown needs more places that serve good food beyond typical business hours, especially with all the hotel guests and people coming in for events.

The counter-service setup keeps things quick, which works for both hotel guests grabbing breakfast and downtown workers looking for lunch. Those custardy eggs and photogenic sandwiches that made them Instagram famous should do well with Atlanta's food scene. Plus, having the first Southern location gives us bragging rights over other cities.
stories.imageAlt: Eggslut bacon, egg and cheese sandwich

stories.headline: Barry's Opens Second Atlanta Location in Midtown's West Peachtree
stories.summary: The high-intensity fitness brand opened Thursday at 1105 West Peachtree St. NW, bringing their signature Red Room workout experience closer to Georgia Tech and the residential towers in Midtown. The studio features the iconic Red Room, a dedicated LIFT studio, Fuel Bar, and premium locker rooms with Dyson hair dryers.
stories.body:
Barry's picking Midtown for their second Atlanta location makes total sense when you look at the neighborhood. You've got Georgia Tech right there, plus all those residential towers full of people who probably want a premium fitness experience within walking distance.

The 1105 West Peachtree development is turning into quite the destination. You've already got Google's Atlanta office, the Epicurean hotel, and several restaurants. Adding Barry's just makes the whole mixed-use concept work better for people living and working in the area.

General manager Mo Fitzpatrick called the location obvious given the proximity to Tech and residential, which shows they actually thought about the market instead of just throwing a pin in a map. That Red Room experience with the cinematic lighting and high-intensity training should fit right in with Midtown's vibe.
stories.imageAlt: Barry's Midtown Atlanta studio grand opening

stories.headline: Georgia Senate Approves Fourth Round of Tax Rebates
stories.summary: The Senate gave final passage Monday to HB 1000, providing up to $500 in income tax rebates for Georgians who filed returns in both 2024 and 2025. Single filers get $250, heads of household receive $375, and married couples filing jointly get $500. The Georgia Department of Revenue will determine when to issue the payments.
stories.body:
Here we go again with the tax rebates. The Georgia Senate unanimously passed the fourth round Monday, continuing what's becoming a yearly tradition of returning surplus funds to taxpayers.

The amounts are the same as previous rounds: $250 for single filers, $375 for heads of household, and $500 for married couples filing jointly. You need to have filed state returns in both 2024 and 2025 to qualify, which should cover most working Georgians.

Governor Kemp's clearly happy about this, posting on social media about providing relief to "hardworking Georgians." Whether these one-time payments actually provide meaningful relief versus ongoing tax reform is debatable, but extra money is extra money. The Department of Revenue will figure out timing and logistics for getting the payments out.
